title: Poets and Pancakes by Asokamitran
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Humor and irony in the film world. The chapter shows the gap between art and commercial cinema and between bureaucracy and creativity.

summary:
This is a humorous memoir of Asokamitran’s time at Gemini Studios, one of the most famous film production companies in Madras in the 1940s and 1950s. “Pancake” was the brand name of the makeup material used heavily at the studio. Actors were covered with it under blazing lights. The author worked in a cubicle cutting newspaper clippings, a job most people thought was useless.

He describes the office boy in the makeup department who blamed Subbu, the number two at Gemini, for all his misery. Subbu was a Brahmin, a poet, an actor, and completely loyal to the Boss. He was creative, charitable, and could suggest many solutions for a film scene. The legal adviser was the opposite; he ended careers through legal pronouncements and wore odd pants.

The studio had a vast and diverse staff including communists, lawyers, poets, and writers, yet most did little creative work. The most notable event was the visit of the English poet Stephen Spender in 1952. No one understood why he came or what he said because his accent and poetry baffled everyone. Years later, the author realized that Spender was editor of Encounter, a magazine connected with anti-communist propaganda, and that the visit had political meaning.

The chapter ends with reference to The God That Failed, a collection of essays by writers disillusioned with communism. The author contrasts the grand literary world with the trivial reality of Gemini Studios. The piece satirizes the film industry, bureaucracy, and the way art often gets lost in chaos and pretension.

qa.q: What does the writer mean by 'the fiery misery' of those subjected to make-up?
qa.a: The make-up room had bright incandescent lights all around, which made the process of getting made up very uncomfortable and hot for the actors.

qa.q: What is the example of national integration that the author refers to?
qa.a: The make-up department had people from different states and communities — Maharashtrian, Kannadiga, Andhra, Madras Christian, Anglo-Burmese, and Tamils — all working together, showing integration even before it became a national slogan.

qa.q: What work did the 'office boy' do in the Gemini Studios? Why did he join the studios? Why was he disappointed?
qa.a: The office boy applied make-up to the crowd players. He had joined hoping to become a star actor, writer, or director, but ended up doing this minor job, which left him disappointed and frustrated.

qa.q: Why did the author appear to be doing nothing at the studios?
qa.a: His job was to cut out and file newspaper clippings, which looked like an insignificant task, making others think he was idle.

qa.q: Why was the office boy frustrated? Who did he show his anger on?
qa.a: He was frustrated because his dream of becoming a star had failed and he was stuck with a lowly job. He directed his anger and frustration at Kothamangalam Subbu, blaming him for his troubles.

qa.q: Who was Subbu's principal?
qa.a: Subbu's principal was The Boss, S.S. Vasan, the head of Gemini Studios.

qa.q: Subbu is described as a many-sided genius.
qa.a: List four of his special abilities. He was a poet, a story writer, a capable actor, and a loyal, resourceful aide who could generate endless creative ideas for the producer.

qa.q: Why was the legal adviser referred to as the opposite by others?
qa.a: Because he was officially called the "legal adviser," but people sarcastically called him "the opposite," implying he gave the wrong or unhelpful advice.

qa.q: What made the lawyer stand out from the others at Gemini Studios?
qa.a: While everyone else wore khadi dhoti and shirt, the lawyer wore pants, a tie, and sometimes a coat, making him look different — a man of logic among dreamers.

qa.q: Did the people at Gemini Studios have any particular political affiliations?
qa.a: No, they mostly wore khadi and admired Gandhi outwardly, but had no real understanding of political ideologies, and were generally suspicious of Communism.

qa.q: Why was the Moral Re-Armament Army welcomed at the Studios?
qa.a: Because Gemini Studios, run by Vasan, was anti-Communist in sentiment, and the MRA was seen as a counter-movement to international Communism, so they were welcomed warmly.

qa.q: Name one example to show that Gemini Studios was influenced by the plays staged by MRA.
qa.a: After watching MRA's play "Jotham Valley," many Tamil plays began copying its style — using a bare stage, a sunrise/sunset scene with a white backdrop and flute music.

qa.q: Who was The Boss of Gemini Studios?
qa.a: S.S. Vasan, the founder of Gemini Studios.

qa.q: What caused the lack of communication between the Englishman and the people at Gemini Studios?
qa.a: The Englishman's strong accent and the audience's unfamiliarity with English poetry and literature created a communication gap; nobody understood what he was talking about.

qa.q: Why is the Englishman's visit referred to as unexplained mystery?
qa.a: Because nobody at the studio knew who he really was or why an English poet/editor was visiting a Tamil film studio — it made no sense to anyone at the time.

qa.q: Who was the English visitor to the studios?
qa.a: He was Stephen Spender, an English poet.

qa.q: How did the author discover who the English visitor to the studios was?
qa.a: Years later, the author came across the magazine The Encounter and recognized Stephen Spender's name as its editor — the same man who had visited the Gemini Studios.

qa.q: What does The God That Failed refer to?
qa.a: It is a book containing essays by six writers, including Stephen Spender, describing their disillusionment with Communism after having once believed in it.

qa.q: The author has used gentle humour to point out human foibles.
qa.a: Pick out instances of this to show how this serves to make the piece interesting. The author humorously describes the make-up department turning "decent-looking" people into "hideous crimson hued monsters," calls the legal adviser's coat "a coat of mail," and pokes fun at the confusion over the mysterious English visitor. This light, witty tone makes ordinary human weaknesses — vanity, pretension, ignorance — entertaining rather than harsh.

qa.q: Why was Kothamangalam Subbu considered No. 2 in Gemini Studios?
qa.a: Subbu was extremely talented, loyal, and resourceful — he could generate countless creative ideas instantly for the producer, was a skilled poet, writer and actor, and was very close to The Boss, making him second-in-command.

qa.q: How does the author describe the incongruity of an English poet addressing the audience at Gemini Studios?
qa.a: The author finds it absurd that an English poet (Stephen Spender) was speaking to a Tamil film studio audience who knew nothing about English poetry and could not even understand his accent — making the event feel completely out of place.

qa.q: What do you understand about the author's literary inclinations from the account?
qa.a: The author appears to have genuine literary interests — he reads English periodicals like The Encounter, recognizes literary names like Stephen Spender, and shows curiosity about ideas like Communism, suggesting he was more well-read and reflective than his humble job at the studio suggested.
